description: This is an isothermal phase diagram of the Fe–20Mn–Al–C system at 1100 °C, with x-axis representing mass percentage of Al (0 to 14 wt.%) and y-axis representing mass percentage of C (0 to ~2.2 wt.%). Five phase regions are clearly marked: single γ (fcc austenite) phase region, M₃C + γ dual-phase region, γ + κ (perovskite κ-carbide) dual-phase region, γ + α (bcc ferrite) dual-phase region, and single α phase region at the highest Al content. Dashed contour lines are estimated iso-density lines, with values ranging from 7.8 g/cm³ at low Al/C content to 6.4 g/cm³ at high Al/C content, showing that alloy density decreases with increasing Al and C contents. This phase diagram supports composition design for low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys, guiding selection of single γ phase compositions with density <7.0 g/cm³ to achieve good strength-ductility matching.

This figure contains two subplots illustrating the correlation of Al (9 to 14 mass%) and C (0 to 2.0 mass%) contents with hardness and cold-workability of Fe-20Mn based low-density alloys. Subplot (a) presents data for quaternary Fe-20Mn-Al-C alloys, and subplot (b) presents data for 5 mass% Cr added quinary Fe-20Mn-Al-C-5Cr alloys. Markers denote identified phase constitutions: open circles for single γ (fcc) phase, solid circles for γ+α (bcc) dual phase, squares for γ+κ (perovskite carbide) dual phase, solid squares for single α phase, and solid triangles for γ+α+κ triple phase only observed in Cr-containing alloys. Each data point is marked with hardness value (Hv) and cold-workability percentage in parentheses, and dashed lines represent hardness isolines. The figure shows that hardness rises with increasing Al and C contents, single γ phase alloys have the best cold-workability, and Cr addition adjusts phase stability regions and property trends, providing guidance for composition optimization of high specific strength low-density steels.

This group of four optical micrographs (all with 50 μm scale bar) present room-temperature microstructures of four Fe–20Mn–Al–C based low-density alloys, all annealed at 1100 °C for 15 min followed by water quenching. Subfigure (a) shows a banded dual-phase microstructure of γ (fcc austenite) and α (bcc ferrite) for Fe–20Mn–10Al–0.5C alloy. Subfigure (b) shows single-phase γ austenite microstructure with annealing twins for Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr alloy. Subfigure (c) shows a tri-phase microstructure containing γ, α, and κ-carbide for Fe–20Mn–13Al–1.8C–5Cr alloy, where the presence of κ phase significantly reduces cold workability. Subfigure (d) shows equiaxed γ+α dual-phase microstructure for Fe–20Mn–13Al–1C–5Cr alloy. These micrographs demonstrate the evolution of phase constitution with Al, C, and Cr contents, provide microstructural evidence for the difference in mechanical properties of the alloys, and support the core conclusion that κ-carbide precipitation and composition regulation enable high specific strength with good ductility in this alloy system.

This figure presents room-temperature engineering stress-strain curves of low-density Fe-20Mn-Al-C based alloys, consisting of two subplots: (a) displays curves for Fe-Mn-Al-C quaternary alloys, covering 5 nominal compositions: Fe-20Mn-10Al-1.5C, Fe-20Mn-13Al-0.75C, Fe-20Mn-11Al-1.0C, Fe-20Mn-10Al-1.0C, and Fe-20Mn-10Al-0.5C; (b) displays curves for Fe-Mn-Al-C-5Cr quinary alloys with 5 wt% Cr addition, covering 4 compositions: Fe-20Mn-11Al-1.8C-5Cr, Fe-20Mn-13Al-1.3C-5Cr, Fe-20Mn-12Al-1.0C-5Cr, and Fe-20Mn-10Al-1.5C-5Cr. It is observed that yield strength and ultimate tensile strength of both alloy systems increase with higher Al and C contents, some high C/Al alloys show a clear upper yield point followed by near-constant flow stress, while tensile elongation ranges from ~20% to ~60%. The results demonstrate that these Fe-20Mn-Al-C(-5Cr) alloys realize excellent strength-ductility synergy derived from nano-sized κ-carbide precipitation hardening, and possess outstanding application potential as low-density high-specific-strength structural materials.

This figure presents two sets of tensile mechanical curves for Fe–20Mn–10Al–5Cr–1.5C alloy, which was annealed at 1100 °C for 15 min followed by two cooling treatments: water quenching (WQ, cooling rate ~1100 °C/s) and air cooling (AC, cooling rate ~15 °C/s). Subplot (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves: the AC sample exhibits higher yield strength, an obvious upper yield point, nearly constant flow stress after yielding, ultimate tensile strength ~1070 MPa, and elongation to failure ~40%; the WQ sample shows gradual work hardening, ultimate tensile strength ~930 MPa, and much higher elongation of ~66%. Subplot (b) shows corresponding true stress-true strain curves: the AC sample has nearly zero work hardening rate at plastic strain below 5% before work hardening rises, with peak true stress ~1440 MPa at true strain ~35%; the WQ sample presents nearly linear work hardening, peak true stress ~1540 MPa at true strain ~52%. The performance difference is attributed to different volume fractions of nanoscale κ-carbide precipitates formed during cooling, proving cooling rate can effectively tailor the strength-ductility tradeoff of this low-density alloy system.

This figure presents TEM characterizations of Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C–5Cr alloys under different cooling conditions. Subfigures (a) and (b) are selected area electron diffraction patterns from water-quenched and air-cooled specimens respectively, with indexed diffraction spots including 000 (transmitted spot), 100 (superlattice reflection of κ-carbide), 200 and 020 (matrix reflections). Subfigures (c) and (d) are dark-field images collected using the (100)κ superlattice reflection, both with a 20 nm scale bar: (c) from the water-quenched sample shows finer, uniformly dispersed nanoscale κ-carbide precipitates, while (d) from the air-cooled sample exhibits larger cuboidal κ-carbide precipitates aligned along <100> and <010> matrix directions. The comparison confirms slower cooling rate (air cooling) promotes growth and oriented arrangement of κ-carbide, supporting the core conclusion that nano κ-carbide precipitation strengthening accounts for the high strength of the studied low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C based alloys.

This figure contains two subplots of tensile strength (MPa) versus elongation (%) for low-density Fe-20Mn-Al-C(-5Cr) alloys. Subplot (a) presents properties of Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C–5Cr γ alloys annealed at 900℃, 1000℃, 1100℃ followed by water quenching (WQ) or air cooling (AC). Air cooling leads to higher tensile strength but lower elongation than WQ at the same annealing temperature, and strength increases as annealing temperature decreases. Subplot (b) shows properties of Fe–20Mn–Al–C(–5Cr) alloys with varied C/Al/Cr contents under as-quenched, 30% cold-rolled, and >90% cold-rolled conditions. Higher cold rolling reduction drastically improves tensile strength while reducing elongation: as-quenched samples show 800–1200 MPa strength with up to ~65% elongation, >90% cold-rolled samples achieve 1800–2100 MPa strength with <5% elongation. The figure confirms cooling route and cold deformation effectively tune the strength-ductility trade-off of these alloys, with ultrahigh specific strength obtained via heavy cold rolling.

This figure plots the relationship between specific strength (unit: MPa·cm³/g, y-axis, range 0-350) and tensile elongation (unit: %, x-axis, range 0-90) for various steel categories. Data points include present low-density Fe–20Mn–Al–C(-Cr) alloys (black dots labeled as Present data), plus conventional steel types: ferritic steels, precipitation hardened steels, super toughness steels, TWIP steels, austenitic steels, martensitic steels, dual-phase steels, and TRIP steels, each marked with distinct symbols. A dashed line indicates the approximate upper limit of specific strength for existing conventional steels, showing an overall decreasing trend of specific strength as elongation increases. The developed low-density alloys exhibit outstanding strength-ductility synergy: many data points lie above the upper limit of conventional steels, with maximum specific strength exceeding 300 MPa·cm³/g, and some samples retain over 180 MPa·cm³/g specific strength at ~40% elongation, confirming their superior specific strength performance relative to traditional steels.
